On an origins 'summons taken out by respondents and directed to S, and appellant, ai for an order for possession of the hotel : Held, reversing the decision of the Supreme Conrt of Tasmania, th the bankruptcy of S$. the appellant was entitled to enforce the proviso forfeiture not only as against S. the lessee, but also as against respondents i equitable mortgages, and that the mere fact of knowledge by the «PP OF AUSTRALIA. 299 tut the le intended to mortgage the term when ereated did not of itself H.C. o A. Auda farther :—That the Equity Procedure Act (No. 4) (Tasmania), (57 Viet., 'Yo. 13) applies only to cases where the rights of the pacties, or of those in Jon under them, are regulated by a mortgage either legal or equitable, and, therefore, that the legal right of a lessor to re-enter under the conditions fa lease eannot be litigated either on equitable or legal grounds on an origi'ating summons by a mortgagee of the term for foreclosure against the lessee. '{ymar from an order of the Supreme Court of Tasmania. On 7th December, 1903, the respondents took out an origi'wating summons for a declaration that they were entitled to posession of a hotel at Queenstown, in Tasmania, known as the "YWeiropole Hotel, and the hall adjoining, known as Cairns' Hall, and also for general relief. The appellant and one Sibley were "uae defendants.

The faets of the case are fully stated in the judgment of Grireira C.J. On llth January, 1904, the respondents obtained the order 'sked for in the summons. This order was affirmed on appeal by "the Full Court of Tasmania; and the present appeal was made a the grounds :—(i.) 'That the Supreme Court was wrong in law 'nholding that the Judge in Chambers had jurisdiction under the Equity Procedure Act (No. 4) (57 Viet., No. 13) to make the der dated 5th January, 1904, and (ii.) that the Supreme Court 'was wrong in deciding that the respondents were entitled to equitable relief against a legal forfeiture of the term of years ganted to Sibley and mortgaged by him to the respondents.

Zodge, for the respondents, moved that the appeal be dismissed. The mortgage by the appellant has been foreclosed, and therefore his no interest in the subject-matter of the appeal. 'The eirnee that a person has been made a party to a suit in the Cont below, if improperly so made, will not entitle him to appeal tadecree made in that suit: Rochfort v. Battersby (1). (Coxon J.—There is a time, viz., between Sibley's insolvency (1) 2 HLL.C., 388. 300 HIGH COURT eas aaa Gamxs Thatis so, pons ax» GRIFFITH CJ.—It is clear that the appellant retains an in Omens. and the question is one of amount.] — Per Cwriam.—The appealable interest remains, and the ape must therefore be heard.

Hudson (with him Banks Smith), for appellant. Under th mortgage from Sibley, the respondents acquired no rights as against the appellant. The agreement for a loan of £400 from. respondents to Sibley was an independent transaction to whi appellant was not a party. 'The lease from appellant to contained a covenant not to sublet without leave in writing a proviso for re-entry on the bankruptey of the lessee; and was prepared by a solicitor acting for both parties. 'The respondent cannot now be heard to say that the lease was not in accordanee with their agreement with Sibley after allowing him to rem in possession under it for more than eighteen months, This sum mons was taken out under the Equity Procedure Act (51 Viet, No. 13), see. 3, under which only the mortgagor and mortgagee and those claiming under them can be made parties. 'The appl lant being the lessor to the mortgagor held a superior title, and being no party to the mortgage transaction, could not be boun by the order of Clark J. [GrirrirH C.J.—Has not the Court a general power to one service of the summons on any parties interested in the subject matter 7] 'The Court must first have jurisdiction to hear the ease ot originating summons.

The only order which could have beet made here was for delivery of possession by the mortgagor. This order could not bind the appellant. 'The originating summons sets out no case against the appellant, and any order made ont, if binding on the appellant, would be a hardship. The Conveyancing and Law of Property Act (Tasmatis) (47 Viet., No. 19), sec. 16 (2) enacts: " Where a lessor is pt ng by action or otherwise, to enforce such a right of re-entry of fo feifure" (iz, under any proviso or stipulation in a lease) "the OF AUSTRALIA. 301 Jase may, in the lessor's action if any, or in any action brought H.

C. or a. "jy himself apply to the Court for relief," and the Court may 1% sai sch relief, having regard to the proceedings and conduct Carns of the parties as it thinks fit. This section does not extend to a Bigoeeaih 'oultion for forfeiture on the bankruptey of the lessee. It has Onsxas. 'een held that an originating summons is not an action or pro"ceeding within its meaning: Lock v.

Pearce (1). That was a "ecsion under the Conveyancing Act, and this is an d fortiori 'as, being an action founded on a mere personal equity. 'There jsno equity against the appellant and, therefore, even if she had Jen rightly joined, no order could have been made against her. "Buist against forfeiture of a lease has never been granted on "originating summons. Julye, for respondents.

Lock v. Pearce (1) decided that what yas really equivalent to an injunction could not be granted on "originating summons. That was in accordance with the English -pnetice, 'The Tasmanian Statute gives more extensive powers cinlief, If appellant had been in pos uve been made against her, as she held under a superior title to thitof the mortgagor.

The Equity Procedwre Act (No. 4) (57 Viet, No, 13) enables the Judge to make an order affecting third pauties. Persons having distinct and separate titles can be "brought before the Court by orginating summons: I'rigfett v. ? Tnion Bank of Australia (2). ; (Gnirerrx C.J.—In that case the other persons joined as defindants were persons entitled to redeem, They were therefore _ Fupetly brought before the Court.] _ Thequestion in this case is, not who is entitled as between uoigagor and mortgagee, but whether the respondents are fnlitled-as against the appellant and Sibley.

A Court of Appeal 'slow to depart from the ordinary procedure of the Court below : _Maggoner Y. Flack (3). If appellant had given her consent to the -'Sigmment there could have been no forfeiture. 'The respondents have aright to an injunction to prevent her from enforeing a {inteture which in equity she could not enforce. 'The equitable ssion, no order could : (0) 2 Ch,, 271 ; 68 Lur., 569, (2) Tasmanian Digest, col. 84. (5) 188 U.S.R., 595. '302 HIGH COURT H.C.or A. mortgagees were really assignees of the lessee.

The ap 1905. had notice through her husband that the respondents were to Cams security over the lease in consideration of £400 to be ad Bonons ax» bY them. This security was worthless with a condition of fy Ormms. feiture in the event of bankruptcy in the lease. "a [Barton J.—Are we not brought back to the question whether it is a usual covenant or not ?] Whether that be so or not, the respondents were at lex entitled to a modified form of the covenant, giving the lessee right to propose a suitable assignee at any time, and then th respondents would have been protected: Hampshire v. Wiekew (1).

The solicitor who drew up the lease acted for both partis, and the appellant consented to the instructions given him by Sibley to protect the rights of the mortgagee. [Gnirrrra C.J.—If you are treating this as a promise, it mug be remembered that the money had been already advaneed a the hotel completed before it was given.] The appellant consented to the equitable mortgage being givey, and is therefore in the position of one who encouraged anothe in the belief that he was to have an interest in the land. [Grirrira C.J—Do you put your case on the ground od fraud 7] Yes; the respondents were led to believe that they weretohave an assignment of the lease. The appellant was privy to the arrangement between Sibley and the respondents, and by het conduct has precluded herself from disputing the respondent rights under their mortgage : Willmott v. Barber (2).

A mutual understanding between parties forms the foundation of an equit able right where one party has knowledge that the other had incurred expense on the faith of that understanding: Bankart\ Tennant (3); Sutherland v. Briggs (4); Helling v. Lumley (3); Plimmer v.

This is an appeal from an order of the Full — jis), 'Cont of Tasmania, affirming an order of Clark J. in Chambers, iy which the appellant and another were ordered to deliver pos'esion of an hotel at Queenstown known as the Metropole Hotel, ofa hall known as " Cairns' Hall," to the respondents " as the "yesons entitled to an assignment of the lease of the said hotel and of the agreement for the tenancy of the said hall." The order 'purports to be made under the Hquity Procedure Act (No. 4) (57 Viet. No. 13), which provides (see. 3) that any mortgagee or mort'gagor may take out, as of course, an originating summons returnable before a Judge sitting in Chambers for such relief of any of the natures or kinds following as may by the summons be specified oras the cireumstances of the case may require, that is to say = "Sale, foreclosure, delivery of possession by the mortgagor, redemption, reconveyance, or delivery of possession by the mort'gee, or account of rents and profits by the mortgagee." Sec. 4 provides that the persons to be served with any summons ander the last preceding section shall be such persons as under the existing practice for the time being of the Supreme Court in its Uuity jurisdiction would be the proper defendants to a suit or 'ution for the like relief as that specified by the summons. The sation also provides that the Judge may direct such other persons 'tobe served with the summons as he may think fit.

The respondents were equitable mortgages by deposit, with a 'written memorandum, of a lease of the hotel in question which is 'hited 19th December, 1901, and was granted by the appellant to te Sibley for a term of four years from Ist November, 1901, and 'fan agreement dated 18th November, 1901, by which she agreed 'grant to Sibley a lease of the Cairns' Hall, which was on an Aljoiing piece of land, for a term of five years from Ist November. Onith December, 1903, the respondents took out their originating (1) 2. Q.B., 218; 76 L.T., 632 1905. — Carexs BURGESS AND Omens.

Grimth ©. HIGH COURT Cairns' Hall, and such further and other relief as they might entitled to. 'The summons was addressed to Sibley the mortg and to the appellant. On 2ist December an affidavit was filed by the respondents iy support of the summons, setting out that before August, 1901, the appellant had begun the erection of an hotel, but was unable complete it, and that thereupon it was agreed between her Sibley that the latter should advance to her £600 to enable her to complete the hotel; that in consideration of the advance thi appellant was to grant to Sibley a lease of the hotel for fou years at a rental of £100 a year with an option of renewal; that Sibley, being unable to provide the whole of the £600, applied the respondents for an advance of £400, which they agreed to make upon an agreement that, as soon as the lease was grantel, Sibley should give them a mortgage of the hotel for the term to seeure his advance ; and that the appellant was aware of that agreement.

The agreement between the appellant and Sibley for the lease of the hotel, which was dated 31st August, 1901, aul the agreement between Sibley and the respondents, which wis dated 22nd October, were exhibited to the affidavit. 'The agreement for the lease stipulated that it should contain all usual covenants and provisoes inserted in leases of publie houses. The affidavit proceeded to set out that the lease was granted by the appellant to Sibley on 19th December, 1901, and was deposited with the respondent as security for the advance of £400, but that no legal mortgage of the term was given; that the lease was not submitted to the respondents for approval, aul they did not admit that it was in all respects in accordance with: the agreement of 31st August. It then set out the agreement of 18th November, 1901, by which the appellant agreed to grant to Sibley a lease for five years of Cairns' Hall at a rental of $4 week, and alleged that this agreement was deposited with beg respondents about 22nd June, 1903, as security for money owilgi and to become owing, by Sibley to them. 'The affidavit proow#lél to show that Sibley had made default in payment of the set LR] OF AUSTRALIA. 'rthe lease of the hotel; that the appellant had refused to ptthe rent from the respondents; that Sibley had made dei in yayment of the £400 and interest; that on 24th 'ember, 1901, he had filed a petition for liquidation of his afiirs by arrangement, and that the first meeting of ereditors had enlveld, but no trustee had been appointed ; that the appellant hal since the Ist December sought to obtain possession of the Jand the contents of the hall; that Sibley had since that iay boon selling liquor in the hotel, ostensibly on his own behalf, jut really on behalf of the appellant ; that the respondents were in possession of part of the hotel by their bailiff, but that the sppallant threatened to eject them ; and that Sibley had refused 'ogive up possession of either the hotel or hall.

It concluded with the following statement:—* My said firm are willing to cept an assignment of the said lease for the residue of the term thereby granted." The lease was not exhibited to the respondents' affidavit, but wasprodueed by the appellant at the hearing of the summons -Tteontained a covenant not to assign or sub-let without the mitten assent of the lessor, and a proviso for re-entry in case of "the lessee making default in payment of rent, becoming bankmuptor filing a petition for liquidation. No evidence was offered eiitw whether these were usual covenants 'and' provinces to be inerted in leases of public houses in 'Tasmania, the only reference tothesubject being that already stated—that the respondents did 'tadmit that the lease was in all respects in accordance with the agreement of 31st August. The agreement for a lease of (sims Hall contained an express stipulation that the lease to be gated should contain a similar covenant and proviso. The appellant's solicitor made an affidavit, which was not niradicted, in which he deposed that Sibley was adjudicated tankrupt on 21st December, and that the appellant had on 27th Amvember taken possession of the hotel and hall under the proeos for forfeiture contained in the lease and agreement.

Ttis apparent on these facts that the appellant had a legal ito re-enter under the lease, and a corresponding right Uuler the agreement of 18th November. Atthe hearing of the summons it was objected for the appelH.C. or A. 1905, — Careys. BURGESS AND Orns.

Gritith C.J. 306 HIGH COURT 1003. equine her to give up poss ' xy It was contended that the jurisdiction extended only to mak Sen iii order against the mortgagor and persons claiming under hin, (Onmexs not to persons claiming possession of the land under a macs title The learned Judge of first instance held that he had juris diction, and the Full Court, with some doubt on this putt affirmed his decision. I will deal first with the objection toth jurisdiction, and will afterwards consider the nature of the « Caress set up against the appellant. 'The Act directs that the originating summons shall be ser on the persons who would be the proper defendants to a suit f the like relief. Now the proper defendants to a suit byaneuit able mortgagee for foreclosure are the mortgagor and all othe persons having a right to redeem.

The appellant is the lessor of the mortgagor. The dealings of a lessee with other cannot prejudice the lessor's rights reserved under the lease, Iith bring about a forfeiture of the lease he can take advantage of fi If they do not, he is not affected. An equitable mortgage of lease is not of itself a breach of a covenant not to assign without leave, although an order for foreclosure has that effect, The lessor ean therefore look with tranquillity upon the litigation, an is free to take advantage of the order of foreclosure or not, as may think fit.

Tt cannot be suggested that the lessor is a neoessiry_ and proper party to a suit for foreclosure of a term. It may by indeed, that a bill for foreclosure which joined the lessor as defendant, and claimed possession of the land as against him on equitable grounds, would be bad for multifariousness. How then can the appellantbe madethe respondentto an originating. 'summons for the same purpose? For the respondents it is contended that the words of see. 4 of the Act, which empower the Judge to diredt the summons to be served on such other persons as he may think fit, confer a general jurisdiction on the Court in these proceedings to determine the right of possession against any person on ally ground.

In my opinion this power is given in aid of the jurislie tion conferred by sec. 3, and does not extend that jurisdietion which is confined to making an order for possession against the mortgagor and persons claiming under him. We were refemel R] OF AUSTRALIA. 307 theeawe of Trifett v. Union Bank of Australia, decided by H.C. or A.

Iate Chief Justice Sir W. Z. Dobson in 1895 (1), in which he is 19% sidtohave exercised jurisdiction under the Kywity Procedwre Act Carne Yo. 4 against claimants of the mortgaged land not claiming porary ap yu the mortgagor.

On examination of that ease, however, OnHens. 'vil be found that no objection was taken to the jurisdiction, crmnos. further that, in the view which the learned Chief Justice tuk of the facts, the claimants derived title from the mortgagor, and were clearly persons entitled to redeem against the mortgages. Inmy opinion the objection to the jurisdiction was well founded. Tithink that the legal right of a lessor to re-enter under the conditions of a lease cannot be litigated on a sumnmons by a mortgagee 'ofthe term for foreclosure, either on equitable or legal grounds, This is sufficient to dispose of the case.

But, as the merits of the ease were argued before us, I will proceed to deal with them. Iwill then become apparent that the objection to the iti isnota mere question of form or procedure, but involves important principles affecting the administration of justice. As presented to us, the respondents' case may be shortly stated thus:—The lessor before the execution of the lease was aware that _ tle respondents were about to advance to the lessee on the security ~ ithe lease a sum of money to enable him to pay to her an agreed sum of larger amount by way of foregift, which was to be applied tyler in erecting buildings on the land.

This knowledge, it was 'tontended, imposed upon the lessor an equitable obligation to grant thelease in such a form that the intending mortgagees would be prtected against any possible forfeiture of the lease to their prelic, or else an equitable obligation not to take advantage of any 'renant or condition in the lease which might have that effect, Tisclear that the mere fact of knowledge by a lessor that the laste intends to mortgage the term when created does not, of 'tli, impose any obligation upon the lessor to protect the future trtgagee's interests. 'The suggested equity must therefore be founded upon some dealings, contractual or other, between the "appellant and the respondents. No evidence whatever was offered sfany contractual relationship between them. The respondents' case 'ust then rest upon the doctrine of estoppel by conduct, and this (1) Tasmanian Digest, col. $4.

HIGH COURT isthe viewwhich the Supreme Court apparently took of it, dence relied upon by them was a passage in the evidence of Si, which, as recorded in the Judge's notes, is as follows :—*Ttold yy, Cairns (appellant's husband) before I paid the £600 that Ba Bros (respondents) had supplied £400 of it, and that I found the security.

This was told by me to him before the lease executed. I told Mr. Cruickshank that Burgess Bros. were findiy £400, and urged him to be careful in drawing the lease so ast protect us." The learned Chief Justice, who delivered the jug ment of the Court, referring to this last piece of evidence, said "She" (the appellant) "must be held to have consented to the instructions given by Sibley to the solicitor that the lease was to be so drafted that the respondents, as equitable mortga, should be protected by it." I do not read the evidence as dix closing any such instructions, but, if they were given by the lessee, I fail to see any evidence of authority in Sibley or Cai to bind the appellant to any departure from the terms of the — agreement of 31st August.

The learned Chief Justice proceeded: — —* She allowed the respondents to advance their money upon these conditions, and she received that money as part of the £600. There is an equitable obligation on the part of the appellant to the respondents not to enforce the condition of forfeiture upam the bankruptey of Sibley, so long as the respondents are prepared to comply with all the provisions of the lease. This obligation is clearly enforceable against the appellant." He then quoted from the language of Lord Chancellor Campbell in Cairneross¥. Lorimer (1) which was a case of equitable estoppel.

Tt has beet often pointed out that the basis of the doctrine of equitable estoppel is fraud. 'The rules of law governing it are clearly statel by Fry J.in Willmott v. Barber (2). Upon these facts 1 am quite unable to see how it can be contended either that the appellant owed any duty to the respondents, or that she mall any representation of an existing fact to them, or that amy representation made was untrue.

The appellant and the respon ents had no dealings with one another. 'The respondents advanoed (1) 3 Macq. H.L. Cas., 827, at p. 830. (2) 15 Cb, D., 96 OF AUSTRALIA. jz money on the security of a deposit of the agreement of August, to be followed by deposit of the lease. 'The agree mt to give the security, which was in writing, is dated 22nd jr, and recites the agreement of 31st August.

The respondats must therefore be taken to have been aware of its terms, ich, so far as the evidence shows, were not departed from. If fherepondents had preferred to take a legal mortgage by assigntor underlease, the consent in writing of the lessor would "juve been necessary to its validity. If they doubted whether {hisassent could be obtained, they might have protected them"glves by obtaining a promise of consent from the appellant "efor advancing their money. They, however, preferred to take "mejuitable mortgage, which did not impose on them any liability tothe lessor, as an assignment would have done.

How then is it "fnufulent for the lessor to take advantage of her legal rights "under the lease? It is not unimportant to observe that when the "rspondents obtained the lease itself, apparently soon after it was scented, and when they must be taken to have known its terms, they made no objection to them. 'The delay was not explained. The summons was to obtain delivery of possession of the land.

Bat the respondents could not ask for possession of more than "jas comprised in their mortgage, that is, the term created by the Jase, which was the foundation of their title, and they were only talitlel to it subject to its conditions. It was suggested that 'tle lease was wrongly drawn, and ought to be treated as if "Tetitied, by omitting the covenant not to assign or sublet with'et Ieeve, and the proviso for re-entry on bankruptey. 'This sigestion, however, has no application to the agreement for a of the Cairns' Hall, which contained express stipulations on 'oth points, Ifa bill had been filed against the appellant for tlenief given by the Court, it would have been necessary to ut plainly the nature of the representations of fact alleged fave been made by her upon which the claim to relief was d, and, if rectification was prayed, to set out the grounds hand nature of, the rectification asked for. Mr.

Lodge was ble to distinctly formulate the supposed representations. 'The ndents, as already pointed out, had, when they made their "mes, full knowledge of the actual agreement between the Youn, fe H.C. or A. 190; — Caress: BURGESS AND Omens. Gritith C.J. H.C. or. appellant and Sibley, and there is no suggestion on the pe y s' 1906.
Buxexss axp Onneers. Grimth C.J. HIGH COURT that the appellant made any repre: entation to the contrary.

Tt is contrary to natural jus party without giving him a proper opportunity of knowing case intended to be made against him. This rule is espee; applicable when the case rests on fraud. In Wallingind Mutual Society (1), Lord Selborne said :—" With regard to fag general allegations, however strong may be the words in whid) they are stated, are insufficient even to amount to an averment fraud of which any Court ought to take notice.

And here fin nothing but perfectly general and vague allegations of fraud, ) would enable any Court to understand what it was that alleged to be fraudulent. These allegations, I think, must | entirely disregarded." facts suggesting fraud, nor does it set up any case for rectfa tion of the lease, If it had done so, the appellant would, at Court had had jurisdiction to entertain the question upon th summons, to set up without proper warning to the appellant acase as that upon which they now rely. I think also that ther was no evidence to support that ease. 'The appeal must the be allowed.

The orders appealed from will be discharged and t summons will be dismissed as against the appellant with in both Courts including costs of Counsel in Chambers. respondents must pay the costs of the appeal. : Barrow J. I coneur. (1) 5 App. Cas., 685, at p. 697.

OF AUSTRALIA. pcosxor J. 'The facts which raise the question of jurisdiction vesinple and beyond dispute. Sibley and Mrs. Cairns were th actualy living on the premises when the proceedings before fe Justice Clark began.

Whether Sibley was there as a lessee anexisting lease, or merely as servant of Mrs. Cairns, it is that Burgess Bros. were equitable mortgagees of the lease iner which he originally entered, and he was their mortgagor rin default, Mrs. Cairns on the other hand was no party to mortgage, did not claim under the mortgage, nor did there any contractual relation between her and Burgess Bros, position was simply that of an owner of the fee simple enternginto possession of her land on the determination of the lease.

Sibley's lease contained a condition for forfeiture if he should the aid of the Bankruptcy Court during the term. It was ted that Sibley had broken that condition ; but it was con'wiled by the respondents that, as against Burgess Bros., Mrs. was not entitled to enter and take possession, and could wi take advantage of the forfeiture, because to do so under the cieumstances alleged by the plaintiffs would be to act in fraud of Bros, and contrary to the duty which in equity she them. Assuming for the moment that Burgess Bros. have talished a ease which in an ordinary equity suit would justify 'onder for possession against Mrs.

Cairns, I am unable to see such an order could be made upon an originating summons the jurisdiction conferred by the Equity Procedure Act, Itisonly a mortgagor or a mortgagee who can take out an iginating summons under the Act, and the relief which can be 'Giained under the summons is defined and limited by see. 3. Matis the section which gives jurisdiction—the rest of the Act nothing more than ancilla i Hest out in these words "sale, foreclosure, delivery of possesly the mortgagor." 'That is the relief which the mortgagee obtain against the mortgagor. The section then goes on to Hine the relief which can be obtained by the mortgagor against mortgagee—'redemption, reconveyance or delivery of possesbythe mortgagee or accounts of rents and profits against the iigagee." 'The next section then directs that the persons to The limits of the juri 3ll H.C. or A. 1905, Catexs BoRGEss axp Orners, O'Connor J.

HO. or A. 1905. — Caress 'BURGESS AND Orns. OCannor J. HIGH COURT be served with the summons are to be those who in a with the existing equity practice "would be the proper de ants to a suit or action for the like relief as that specifi by summons," thus giving the Court power to bring in persons ql ing under mortgagor or mortgagee, or having such interes the mortgage as the Court would deem necessary to consider protect in making the order. 'Then follow at the end of th tended that these words are wide enough to justify making Mx Cairns a party to these proceedings and giving to Burgess Bm the relief of an award of possession as against her.

Tam unabh to assent to that contention. The words no doubt give the Ju an uncontrolled discretion as to the persons who may be se with the summons and who thereby may get notice of the pr ceedings. It is necessary that he should have wide powers of bringing before him all those persons whose interests must be considered in making the order.
But these powers are in aid the relief which the Judge is authorized to give. The scope of the relief itself is not extended. That is confined by the te of the summons.

The Judge cannot give relief beyond claimed in the summons, and the summons can only claim relief defined in sec. 3, namely relief by a mortgagor agai a mortgagee or by a mortgagee against a mortgagor. There nothing to prevent the Judge directing Mrs. Cairns to be servel with the summons at the risk of costs to Burgess Bros. if th Judge should think fit so to order.

But bringing her before th Court could not give the Court jurisdiction to make an onde against her when she was neither the mortgagor nor claimel under him. If the interpretation contended for by the respondent were adopted, it would be always in the power of the Judge to tum an originating summons underthe Act into a suit for summary¢je ment as against any person in possession of the mortgaged land, no matter how the possession arose, or upon what ground an onde for possession was sought against him. Taking the ordinary gral" matical meaning of the words of the Act, no such jurisdiction given.

But assummg that the general words of the last part oft 4 which have quoted were grammatically capable of being © OF AUSTRALIA. 313 Tempe and purpose of the Act itself will show that such a contraction would be contrary to the intention of the legislature. Crys "From beginning to end of it the Act expressly deals only with the ponas xp rights of mortgagors and mortgagees. The form of proceeding, Oruers. iy originating summons which, as in this instance, gives no notice oconmora. ly work no injustice in cases where the rights in conare those of mortgagor and mortgagee or of persons chiming under them—rights in most cases determined by deed, 'by agreement, or by consideration of the conditions of an qitable deposit, But where the Judge has to go outside the 'deed or agreement, or the circumstances of the equitable deposit, ni has to investigate the rights of parties who are strangers to he deed or agreement and whose right to possession depends upon utirely different considerations, there is danger of injustice being worked by the application of a summary procedure which gives the party in possession no notice of the case against which he has todefend himself.

Perhaps no stronger illustration of the unfairvss of applying this summary procedure against a defendant in yosession not a party to the mortgage could be given than is afforded by this case itself. One can well understand that the bgislature might have seen good reason for establishing a cheap ani summary method of proceeding in cases between mortgagor 'tl mortgagee. But taking the whole scope and purpose of the AtTean see no indication of an intention on the part of the 'hgislature to extend the jurisdiction of the Act beyond those cases mherethe rights of the parties, or of those in possession under them, a te regulated by a mortgage whether legal or equitable.

If the Upjection now under consideration involved merely a question of metice or procedure, as has been urged by the respondents, this Contt would not interfere if the practice or procedure' were well@ablithed, even though the Court might think the practice or dure irregular or inconvenient. But the question is one of ion, substantive jurisdiction, and not merely of practice Preedure, Besides, the practice or procedure, which the re'ymdents claim they were right in following, has not been by Wy means established. I agree with my learned brother the 3l HIGH COURT H.0 oF A.

Chief Justice that the ease of Triffett v. Union Bank of 1905. tralia (1) does not decide the question, and the judgment of Cams Supreme Court of Tasmania in the present case, although Bonoxs anv ing Mr. Justice Clark's decision does so on this point with Ornexs. doubt.

On this part of the case therefore I have come to order appealed against, and that the Supreme Court of Tas ought to have so held. As to the rest of the case I entirely agm with my learned brother the Chief Justice that no reason hase shown why Mrs Cairns should not as against Burgess Bros, take advantage of the forfeiture of Sibley's lease and re-enter into possession of her land.
